description: Two people in a shared life, close relationship, or paired undertaking may call one another 'tewema'. The word covers romantic and practical partnerships when the participants claim that relation. Phi leaves marriage as source material; the partnership itself carries no gender mark.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into middle-height front unrounded 'e'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move into stressed middle-height front unrounded 'e'. Close the lips for voiced 'm', let the sound resonate through the nose, and finish with open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Stressed 'we' sits at the centre and also opens 'wemi' (ally). The echo makes partner and ally easier to remember as neighbouring relations without turning 'we' into a productive piece of either word.
usage_notes: A plural subject can predicate with the noun, as in 'lo mia tewema nai' (we are partners). The surrounding account states the kind of partnership and whatever commitments its participants have made.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The unmarked form lets people name their relation in their own terms. Gender and institutional status do not arrive before the partnership.

semantic_domains.activity: a partner may share one bounded undertaking with another person
semantic_domains.community: a paired practical or personal relationship
